title: Pocket Inserts and Envelopes for Journals
subtitle: Three pocket types: cut-and-glue, simple envelope fold, and origami full envelope
excerpt: Three techniques for adding storage to a journal page: a glued inside pocket from folded card, a simple two-fold envelope, and a fully enclosed origami-style pocket. No content needed, just paper and basic tools.
sourceNotes: General journalling-craft practice; no single PD canonical source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded strip of paper or card glued to a journal page on three sides to form an open-topped storage pocket.
A sequence of folds that converts a rectangular sheet into a self-contained envelope with a tucked or glued flap.
Any loose piece of paper, card, or fabric held inside a journal, either loose or fixed in a pocket.

attrs.heading: Cutting safely
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

content.text: What you are making

These three pocket formats solve the same problem, loose pieces of paper, cards, or tickets that belong with a journal page but have nowhere to sit. Each has different capacity and construction complexity. Start with Method 1 if you are new to paper craft; it takes about five minutes once you know the fold.
Method 1: Cut-and-glue inside pocket
This is the simplest
inside pocket
: a strip of paper folded and glued at three edges, leaving the top open.
Cut a rectangle of paper the same width as your journal page and roughly half the page height. 90 gsm cartridge paper works well; thin printer paper is fine.
Score a fold line 1 cm from the bottom edge using your bone folder and ruler. Fold that strip up and crease firmly.
Turn the piece over. Apply a thin line of PVA along the left edge, the right edge, and the bottom fold, leaving the top edge unglued.
Press onto the journal page and hold for 30 seconds. Place a heavy book on top and leave to dry for 10 minutes.
Run a strip of washi tape along the top edge of the pocket for a neater finish and to reinforce the opening against tearing.
Method 2: Simple envelope fold
The
envelope fold
uses a single sheet of A5 or A6 paper with no cutting, just folds and one optional glue point.
Lay the sheet landscape. Fold the bottom edge up to meet the top edge. Crease with the bone folder.
Fold the left edge in by about 1.5 cm. Crease.
Fold the right edge in by the same amount, overlapping the left fold at the centre. Crease.
Fold the top flap down to form the envelope closure. Crease well.
Unfold the top flap. Apply a small dot of PVA to the underside of each side flap where it overlaps the base, then re-fold and hold.
This envelope holds flat
journal inserts
such as receipts, cut-out images, or folded notes. The top flap stays open unless you add a washi-tape seal or a small tag.
Method 3: Origami-style full envelope
This method produces a fully enclosed pocket from a single square of paper. The diagonal fold geometry seals on four sides once the point is tucked in.
Cut or fold a square from your sheet (15 cm x 15 cm works well for an A5 journal).
Place the square as a diamond (point at top). Fold the bottom point up to meet the centre. Crease with the bone folder.
Fold the left point to the centre. Crease.
Fold the right point to the centre, overlapping the left fold. Crease.
Fold the top point down so its tip reaches the centre point. This is the flap.
Tuck the tip of the top point under the left/right overlap to lock the envelope shut without glue.
To attach this envelope to a page, apply PVA to the back of the left and right panels only (avoid the flap). Press to the page and dry under weight.
Choosing which method to use

Method 1 is flattest and best for thin inserts that must lie flush when the journal closes. Method 2 handles thicker contents and can be made oversize to hold folded A5 sheets. Method 3 looks neatest but has the least capacity; use it for small cards, stamps, or folded notes.

items.symptom: PVA seeps through thin paper and marks the journal page
items.fix: Use the thinnest possible smear: spread the PVA with a fingertip or a scrap of card so it is translucent before gluing. Alternatively, glue only the very edges, not the whole surface.

items.symptom: Origami envelope corners do not line up neatly
items.cause: The sheet was not perfectly square to begin with
items.fix: Check the square before starting: fold one corner to the opposite corner and check that the edges match. Trim with craft knife and ruler if needed.

items.symptom: Inside pocket tears at the opening after a few uses
items.cause: No reinforcement at the top edge
items.fix: Run a strip of washi tape across the full width of the opening, both inside and outside, before gluing the pocket to the page.
